speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. Survivor and war crimes trials participant is Lawrence Rhee. And the date is April 9, 1996. We left off on the last tape-- I asked you to describe the courtroom. And then you started talking about the, the movie that was done many years ago. What movie were you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it-- uh-- Judg-- yes. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you like to say about the courtroom and about the movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: The courtroom-- it was the very same courtroom. But the-- of course, filmmakers have to have their own script. [LAUGHS] And they have to have their own characters. And I personally was not very well satisfied with, with the picture. Now, other people may have had different--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't what I had experienced.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you experience? First of all, can you describe the courtroom as you saw it? What did it look like, the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's hard to describe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You went into the courtroom. To the left were the-- was the prisoner dock-- docket. And immediately adjacent to them was the place where the interpreters worked There were eight interpreters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And across from the prisoners was the bench for the judges. The eight judges were seated there. Right in front of the prisoners was there defense-- were their defense lawyers. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] across from the defense lawyers, in front of the judges, were the court reporters and court stenographers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right angle to that, facing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's put it this way-- there was the prosecution. The person who was the prosecutor at any particular moment had the judges on his right side and the prisoners, or the accused, on the left side. Behind him were the various tables and desks where the prosecution sat. Each prosecution had their own table. And-- and behind those is where the, the observers were-- they-- the press, on the bottom floor the press, and the visitors' gallery-- gallery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in the court all the time. I was in court only when needed. But sometimes, I sat with the Russian prosecution. You will have a picture later on where you see me with Colonel Petrovski, and sometimes in the projection booth, where I had projections to do, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes at the American prosecution staff table. So wherever evidence was needed where they had it, that's where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you personally see the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who the defendants were? Can you name them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see if memory [LAUGHS] last that long. Of course, there was Goering, Hess, Kaltenbrunner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] von Ribbentrop, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Keitel, Jodl, Donitz, Raeder, von Papen, Schacht, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Speer, Streicher, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Funk, Frank, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Baldur von Schirach. And there's still, like, five. Uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one who succeeded Goebbels. I can't think of-- can't think of-- this is the one that succeeded Goebbels as the propaganda minister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All right, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Maybe you'll think of more names--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As we--

speaker: interviewee
text: As we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go along. What's your recollection when you saw them for the first time? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A moment of satisfaction, [LAUGH] Real satisfaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they look to you? How did they appear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like-- really like ordinary human beings. I mean, just looking at them doesn't mean too much. The military and the navy people were in their uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Goering was in a uniform. But it was not complete. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were dressed in many--

speaker: interviewee
text: The other ones were dressed in civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Heinrich Hoffmann at this time? He was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was in the room where we worked He, he never-- he wa-- he had to go into the courtroom once, as far as I know. He told me he had to go-- this guard took him there-- to swear that the evidence that he-- that was from him was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other delegations? You said there were other delegations present. Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, you had the Russians, the French, the British, and the American. And then they had subdelegations, like the French had-- I mean, the Russian had, for instance, had the Yugoslav, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so forth, and wherever the-- but they were part of the Russian prosecution staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any names of the people on the American delegation? Who was the prosecutor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- Justice Jackson. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ex-senator, Dodd, whose son is senator also in, in Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names in the American delegation you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, yes, there was a fellow by the name of-- he handled Rosenberg's case. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, it'll come back to me after a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the Soviet delegation? Were there any names that stand out to you, the Russian delegation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Colonel Petrovski, with whom I worked quite a bit and have a story to tell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Colonel Petrovski and I had a wonderful working relationship. So nothing personal ever happened. But he spoke German fluently. And all our business was actually transacted in German. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when my contract was up, it was just a few weeks before the trials ended. And my contract was not renewed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted show him how much I had appreciated our working relationship. So I went and bought him a Parker pencil-- pen and pencil set. I went to his office and started to talk German and tell him the reason for my coming. And I handed him this little gift. And suddenly, he switched from speaking German into speaking Russian through an interpreter. I didn't speak Russian. And he-- and, and I, of course, switched to English. And he told something to his interpreter-- secretary interpreter. And she left the room. And a few minutes later, she came back with two volumes of evidence that actually had been presented in court just a few weeks earlier. And he autographed one of them. And then he told me, Mr. Rhee, it has been a pleasure working for you-- with you. If you ever feel the need-- or feel that I can be of any need or help to you, please do not hesitate to write to me care of the Kremlin. Of course, I never did. And that's how I got the Russian evidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the defendants brought in in the trial-- into the courtroom? how-- Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, under guard, every one. They had to go up a little elevator-- came up in a little elevator. And I myself never talked to any of the defendants. I had nothing to do with them. I never saw their quarters or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of the opening statements by either the prosecution or the defense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some, some of them-- not, not the main-- when Justice Jackson opened the, the trials or-- there was not even standing room. And you would have had to be airborne to get in. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you present at that, when he opened that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any opening statements at all that you can describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, not the opening statements so much. I recall some of the personalized opening statements, like when they had Goering as an individual, or some of that, yes, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you recall for us some of those? For example, start with Goering.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they wanted to prove that-- they said, we can prove this and this and that and that but any particular moments. And that, that pertained to all of them, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear Goering speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was smart as a fox. He wiggled out of the questions. He had a way of wriggling around the answers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not really, anymore, but I know that Justice Jackson, at that time, was not very successful in nailing him down. But the English chief prosecutor, Lord-- I think it was-- Lord Maxwell-Fyfe was his name-- was able to nail him. He had a different approach. I mean, I'm not a lawyer, and-- but he had a different way of, let's call it, call it, nailing him down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone else on the English delegation you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- well, we had some business with them. But I do not recall their names now. This is 50 years ago. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other defendants that you recall what they said, beside Goering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mentioned-- well, this was a witness-- [? Olindorf. ?] And I remember one particular incident. This, I did not witness. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hess-- Rudolf Hess's defense had been trying to prove to the court that he was insane-- that he could not be held accountable for the things that were done. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had some psychologists in there, or psychiatrists-- whatever you call them-- on the defense. And they always pleaded that he was innocent. He should be let go. And after all the talking was over, Hess gets up-- or got up-- and said, I am perfectly sane. I want to stand to stick to my buddies with whom I have worked all my life. And I accept all the responsibilities. And that was a bombshell. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually hear him say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I said I, I, I wasn't in there at that time. But I was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any other stories about any of the lead prosecutors? Who were the lead prosecutors? Can you say their names again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- well, Lord Maxwell-Fyfe and Justice Jackson and Dodd, and the other one-- there is one that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned recently has died since. He was on the prosecution staff. But his name escapes me. But I have it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you can recall about the prosecutors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not anything outstanding that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the defense counsel? Can you recall-- how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were involved in the defense, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- each one had at least one defense lawyer. Some of them had two. But just how many, I, I do not recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anybody stand out in particular or any incidents of the defense?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no, I, I really don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were in another room most of the time. And they would call you in as they need you. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were notified in advance-- not just call me in, no. They, they said, tomorrow such and such a thing is going to happen. And we have to have this and this ready, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what specifically, you know, would happen? Can you give me an example--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of when they called you in? And what did you show?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- there's-- I didn't show so much, except that I-- like, the lampshades, I actually put them into the courtroom. I did show stills on the projector. And I did show films on the projector. And we also made up organizational charts to show how the-- any given unit had been organized and who was the head of it. So we could nail on the, the defendant with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me an example of an organizational chart and who you nailed down, using it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was against-- who was in the east? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have some on the pictures, there, but I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whether it was-- was it Sauckel? Or was it Frank? I, I really don't recall. But those were the things we had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made up the organizational--

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they were done by people who knew how to do these things. And we got them into court and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was done by the prosecution?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by the prosecution, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would show who was in charge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your job was to display.

speaker: interviewee
text: To display them, yes, against the wall, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other exact evidence did you present, besides the organizational charts? What else did you present?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pictures, movies taken by, let's say, army-- US Army Signal Corps, of the liberation, of some camps, and of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] burial, if you want to call it-- such digging trenches and then push the bodies in with bulldozers and have lime put on top of them and then cover them up again. The lime, of course, was to make the bodies disintegrate faster.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these-- this evidence that you had came from where? The ones that you showed of the camp-- you know, in the camp, the atrocities--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was US Army-- the American--

speaker: interviewer
text: US Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: US Army Signal Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were provided with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were provided, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And movies were also provided?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you had Yugoslavian evidence and Russian evidence. Did you actually show that, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was actually shown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any memorable witnesses that stand out in your mind, that were at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- Field Marshal Paulus, who had defended Stalingrad. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were, as I remember, around 300,000 German troops there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the Russian came back to attack af-after they had defended Moscow and the Germans were forced to withdraw in the north, the-- they had more troops available. And they, they attacked Stalingrad. And von Paulus [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- saw what the situation was. And being a, an army guy, a military man, he asked Hitler-- now this is his statement that I heard, myself. He asked Hitler to surrender the troops, to save the lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Hitler's response was to send him a pistol to commit suicide. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, 300,000 German guys lost their lives there. But von Paulus got out. And he was a very weak, old man when I saw him in court, walked on a cane, and very skinny, and very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me make sure I understand. I clarify it. His name, again, was von Paulus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Von F-- V-- you want to spell it? V-O-N is one word. That's the German title for the nobility. And Paulus is P-A-U-L-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a German. That's what-- that's what I'm trying--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A field Marshal for the German--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Or was a general? But I think I-- somehow, it sticks in my mind that he was a field marshal.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- were you involved with the International Military Tribunal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the media? What do you recall about the media at this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, other than that they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fooled the guards here with that little ID deal that I told, I never met many of them, really. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: They sat--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- but the only thing I recall is-- this was the Russian media-- they wanted to take a pictures of Hoffmann. And they came in with a captain, a Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] correspondent-- correspondent-- and somebody else. And they took a picture. And I happen to be in that picture. So I can say I appeared in [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else about the photographic evidence, about how it was analyzed or how it was represented? Does anything else come to mind with the photographic-- who actually analy-- I mean, I know you had to present it. But who actually looked over it to determine its authenticity and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of it, as I said, like the evidence in Yugoslavia was actually done by the Germans themselves. The German photographers took the pictures of the atrocities, made hundreds and hundreds of prints, and circulated them through the population to keep the population in a state of being scared. So that evidence was, of course, easy to pick up by the Yugoslav prosecution. Because there were hundreds of them-- thousands of them-- available. Then the each individual armies had their-- excuse me-- their signal corps. When they came-- excuse me-- came to camp or something, they took pictures with it. Then I had this one-- I remember one was done by a, a German amateur movie guy who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] showed the, the way the prisoners were received at a concentration camp-- how they were-- the women were pulled naked by their hair and so forth and so on. You have seen these little stills that I have of there-- of them. That's another one. That was only a very short-- I don't think the whole thing lasted eight minute. and things like that, we, uh-- then also, I remember once I was sent to Berlin to pick up evidence that had been gathered there and bring it back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What evidence was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was documents and some film. I know I had to go there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you mentioned earlier that the Russian had given you photographic evidence. And now how was that authenticated of the atrocities he gave you? Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: How, how, the, the Russians themselves authen-- authenticated it, I really don't know. But it, it, it was official. I mean, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was all this evidence stored during the time of the trial? Where did they keep it all? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got it. And once it got into the court's hands, I don't know. We didn't store it. When we had some, we came up with it. We notified the particular persons who needed it that we had some.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who actually had access to it, besides you? You know, was it under lock and key, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, it was. In fact, I was practically the last man on the line to get it. Where it was kept before that, I, I really don't know. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: After [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It was shown, then where was it, uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened. I guess the either prosecution or maybe the defense got copies of it also. I, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time you saw the photographic evidence and some of the atrocities in the pictures, what was your reaction to that when you saw these pictures for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of it I couldn't believe. I mean, just as you having seen some of it, it, it leaves you a little numb, to say the least.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of effect has it had on you, over time, seeing these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: No other than I like I want the world to know what happened and not to be covered up by some guys-- the so-called revisionists-- that say these things never happened. Oh, yeah, a few thousand might have died. They got sick or this and that. But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the things like that never happened. The Germans were much too cultured to do things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point at all did you have any interaction with any of the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or their attorneys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you present for any of the closing comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had already left, you remember. I-- my contract was up and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you actually in the Nuremberg trial? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, I came in as a GI. And about three, four months later, I took my discharge and then-- in Bamberg-- and then got my was it-- civilian status. And I forgot how long my contract-- I have the paper there, but I never looked at it again. But it was up a few weeks before the actual trials were over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- did you want to stay on? Or what was your feeling about--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I didn't. I felt that I don't-- I had enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other memorable courtroom incidents, either inside or outside, that you can think of that happened to you personally, that you observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. How-- I took lots of pictures clandestinely. And the way I did that-- I was not supposed to take any photographs in the courtroom. But a photograph-- photographer friend of mine took my pictures-- my, my, my camera into the courtroom. I walked into the courtroom. And once inside, of course, I wasn't searched. And, well, he handed me back my camera. And that's how, how I got the pictures.
